category_summary:
**Anthropic's Claude Sonnet 4.6** launch dominated the day. **Mike Krieger** (Anthropic CPO) [announced it approaches](/?date=2026-02-18&category=social#item-a216df5f0fe9) Opus-level intelligence at Sonnet pricing, with [computer use as the standout](/?date=2026-02-18&category=social#item-de5b749a12c8) capability. **Boris Cherny** confirmed it's [live in **Claude Code**](/?date=2026-02-18&category=social#item-815f0360bbd2), and **Perplexity** rolled it out immediately to Pro/Max subscribers.

- The **Figma + Claude Code** integration [generated massive excitement](/?date=2026-02-18&category=social#item-6841cbf132db) (800K+ views), enabling AI-generated UI to flow directly into **Figma** as editable design frames—a concrete milestone for AI-assisted design workflows
- **Andriy Burkov** went viral with a novel finding: simply [repeating a prompt twice](/?date=2026-02-18&category=social#item-98537f1be46a) significantly boosts LLM accuracy across benchmarks, a zero-cost technique that surprised the community
- **John Carmack** [proposed a GPU preemption mechanism](/?date=2026-02-18&category=social#item-dc8bab2ed87f) for research clusters, offering deep technical insight into infrastructure pain points
- **Greg Brockman** [posted a detailed hiring call](/?date=2026-02-18&category=social#item-654aae943e78) for **OpenAI** infrastructure and security engineers, offering rare insider perspective on how AI is reshaping internal engineering
- **Hugo Larochelle** [shared independent research](/?date=2026-02-18&category=social#item-f799efd034ce) replicating **METR's** exponential trend in LLM agent time horizons, while **Nathan Lambert** [analyzed how RL research](/?date=2026-02-18&category=social#item-413f9b058830) in academia is shifting from benchmark-maxing toward more robust methodologies in 2026
- **Lambert** also [offered a contrarian take](/?date=2026-02-18&category=social#item-bb4a50b7ada0): open models are doing better than expected but show no evidence of closing the frontier gap permanently

items.title:
If you’re an infrastructure or security engineer, now is the best time to join OpenAI.

It’s hard no...
items.content:
If you’re an infrastructure or security engineer, now is the best time to join OpenAI.

It’s hard not to be inspired by what today’s coding tools are capable of, and we have line of sight to making them much better. While our core ML infrastructure problems remain much the same as always — training and inferencing models at scale, co-designing end-to-end for maximum effect, managing complexity and maintaining fast iteration — what it feels like to solve these problems in practice is changing fast.

As the models have been improving, our ability to get value out of them is increasingly bottlenecked by thoughtfully designed infrastructure — whether figuring out how to manage agent cross-collaboration, having ergonomic sandboxes that let the agents complete end-to-end workflows securely, building tools/abstractions/observability/frameworks which allow the agents to move faster, and scaling supervision of the agents' work.

Engineering is already different from a few months ago, and will continue to evolve. Having seen many generations of engineering and AI tools, I believe what is most important going forward will be skills like the following: strong understanding of your domain, ability to think through abstraction/architecture/design/how the pieces should fit together, and deep curiosity to explore what these models have to offer.

If you’d like to help us build the future of AI, while using AI to get there, email me: gdb+infra@openai.com. Include a description of a surprising or creative way you’ve gotten value out of the models recently, and your contributions to any project in your career on which you’ve made a significant difference in its outcome. Feel free to include any other context that can help us understand how you operate and the problems you want to work on.

items.title: LLMs process text from left to right — each token can only look back at what came before it, never f...
items.content:
LLMs process text from left to right — each token can only look back at what came before it, never forward. This means that when you write a long prompt with context at the beginning and a question at the end, the model answers the question having "seen" the context, but the context tokens were generated without any awareness of what question was coming. This asymmetry is a basic structural property of how these models work.

The paper asks what happens if you just send the prompt twice in a row, so that every part of the input gets a second pass where it can attend to every other part. The answer is that accuracy goes up across seven different benchmarks and seven different models (from the Gemini, ChatGPT, Claude, and DeepSeek series of LLMs), with no increase in the length of the model's output and no meaningful increase in response time — because processing the input is done in parallel by the hardware anyway.

There are no new losses to compute, no finetuning, no clever prompt engineering beyond the repetition itself. 

The gap between this technique and doing nothing is sometimes small, sometimes large (one model went from 21% to 97% on a task involving finding a name in a list). If you are thinking about how to get better results from these models without paying for longer outputs or slower responses, that's a fairly concrete and low-effort finding.

Read with AI tutor: https://t.co/MipHHO6rjX

Get the PDF: https://t.co/XQrqiaGwIO

items.title: The glory work of GPU scheduling is in the frontier data centers with hundreds of thousands of GPUs,...
items.content:
The glory work of GPU scheduling is in the frontier data centers with hundreds of thousands of GPUs, but a lot of research work is done with single GPU jobs on modest clusters, and the scheduling leaves much to be desired.

I wish there were a clean way to preempt GPU tasks, so long running tasks could be transparently paused to allow higher priority tasks to get the minimum time-to-results. Manual checkpointing and cooperative multitasking is an option, but it complicates codebases and is fertile ground for bugs.

It feels like most of the pieces are present: Everything goes through page tables on the GPUs already, Nvidia UVM (Unified Virtual Memory) allows demand paging to host memory, and MPS (Multi-Process Service) could act as a CUDA shim to force everything to use a different memory allocator. Memory page thrashing would be catastrophic for GPU tasks, but the idea would be to pause the host task of the low priority process, then let the high priority process force only the necessary pages out (or maybe none at all, if the memory pressure wasn’t high enough) while it is running, then resume the low priority task on completion, allowing it to page everything back in. Task switching at the level of tens of seconds, not milliseconds.

Even if it didn’t handle absolutely all memory (kernel allocations and such) and had some overhead, that would be quite useful.

Of course, Nvidia would prefer you to Just Buy More GPUs!
items.author: @ID_AA_Carmack
items.summary: John Carmack discusses GPU scheduling challenges for research clusters, proposing a transparent preemption mechanism using Nvidia UVM and MPS to pause low-priority GPU tasks for higher-priority ones, enabling task switching at tens-of-seconds granularity rather than milliseconds.
items.reasoning: Highly technical, original insight from John Carmack (legendary programmer). Very high engagement (65K views, 895 likes). Identifies a real pain point in GPU resource management for AI research and proposes a concrete architectural solution. The closing quip about Nvidia preferring you 'Just Buy More GPUs' adds industry commentary.
items.summary_html: John Carmack discusses GPU scheduling challenges for research clusters, proposing a transparent preemption mechanism using Nvidia UVM and MPS to pause low-priority GPU tasks for higher-priority ones, enabling task switching at tens-of-seconds granularity rather than milliseconds.

items.content: Ever since @METR_Evals' fascinating work on LLM agent time horizons, I've wanted to see other attempts to draw conclusions from the same data. In a separate approach by Fengyuan and Jay, we too infer an exponential, but with shorter horizons for recent models (~2h vs METR's ~5h)

items.title: RL research in academia is shaping up to be much more exciting in 2026 than the last half of 2025. H...
items.content:
RL research in academia is shaping up to be much more exciting in 2026 than the last half of 2025. Here's why I see it as a healthier transition from a bit of benchmaxing to more interesting and robust research (dare I say RL generalizing across many domains?)

2025 was largely a setup year, where due to the simplicity of the environments, insignificant algorithmic changes could appear more valuable than they were.

Today, substantial work is focusing on algorithms for tool-use, procedural environment generation, and richer data systems. I was noticing this with how there are many "environment" papers back in 2025, but thinking about what the difference is -- today they're more about generalization, diverse tasks, more complex environments.

The first environments were something along the lines of "here's some existing tooling, lets adapt it to be a binary RL problem." Today, papers like endless terminals are setting up very interesting RL data pipelines.

This is at least an interesting complement to the environments industry, where labs will buy 10-20 environments for millions of dollars and get benefit out of a few of them.

Academics working on methods that create RL data and meaningful generalization (robust behaviors) will be way, way more fun to study and integrate than "another algorithm to increase AIME".

Anyways, I'm happy to have figured this out, and if you see more papers understanding generalization across more environments or how clever design can make specialized small models with RL, tag me!
items.author: @natolambert
items.summary: Nathan Lambert provides an insightful analysis of how RL research in academia is evolving in 2026 - shifting from benchmark-maxing to more robust research focused on tool-use algorithms, procedural environment generation, and generalization across domains.
items.reasoning: Excellent original analysis from a leading RL researcher at AI2. High engagement (417 likes, 24K views). Provides a nuanced view of how academic RL research is maturing from simple binary environments to rich data pipelines and generalization. Contrasts academic vs industry approaches.

items.title: It really isn’t that hard to see the jagged frontier of AI. Just think about the parts of your job t...
items.content:
It really isn’t that hard to see the jagged frontier of AI. Just think about the parts of your job that are vital but that you would be insane to expect an AI to do, even if agents get 10x better. Thats the frontier

The more you use AI the more accurate those assessments will be

items.summary: Ethan Mollick discusses the 'jagged frontier' of AI - encouraging people to identify parts of their jobs that AI cannot do even if agents improve 10x, and notes that heavy AI usage improves these assessments.
items.reasoning: Mollick is a highly credible voice on AI adoption. The 'jagged frontier' framing is influential and this extends it with practical advice. Good engagement (178 likes, 14.5K views).

items.title:
The power of AI agents comes from:
1. intelligence of the underlying model
2. how much access you gi...
items.content:
The power of AI agents comes from:
1. intelligence of the underlying model
2. how much access you give it to all your data
3. how much freedom & power you give it to act on your behalf

I think for 2 & 3, security is the biggest problem. And very soon, if not already, security will become THE bottleneck for effectiveness and usefulness of AI agents as a whole (1-3), since intelligence is still rapidly scaling and is no-longer an obvious bottleneck for many use-cases.

The more data & control you give to the AI agent: (A) the more it can help you AND (B) the more it can hurt you.

A lot of tech-savvy folks are in yolo mode right now and optimizing for the former (A - usefulness) over the the latter (B - pain of cyber attacks, leaked data, etc).

I think solving the AI agent security problem is the big blocker for broad adoption. And of course, this is a specific near-term instance of the broader AI safety problem.

All that said, this is a super exciting time to be alive for developers. I constantly have agent loops running on programming & non-programming tasks. I'm actively using Claude Code, Codex, Cursor, and very carefully experimenting with OpenClaw. The only down-side is lack of sleep, and an anxious feeling that everyone feels of always being behind of latest state-of-the-art. But other than that, I'm walking around with a big smile on my face, loving life 🔥❤️

PS: By the way, if your intuition about any of the above is different, please lay out your thoughts on it. And if there are cool projects/approaches I should check out, let me know. I'm in full explore/experiment mode.
items.author: @lexfridman
items.summary: Lex Fridman shares a detailed analysis of AI agent security as the key bottleneck for broad adoption, arguing security > intelligence for many use cases. Also shares his personal experience using Claude Code, Codex, Cursor, and OpenClaw.
items.reasoning: Substantive analysis of AI agent security from a highly visible figure. Very high engagement (3.5K likes, 300K views). The mention of OpenClaw alongside established tools is noteworthy. Identifies security as THE bottleneck for agent adoption.
items.summary_html: Lex Fridman shares a detailed analysis of AI agent security as the key bottleneck for broad adoption, arguing security > intelligence for many use cases. Also shares his personal experience using Claude Code, Codex, Cursor, and OpenClaw.
items.content_html:
The power of AI agents comes from:

1. intelligence of the underlying model

2. how much access you give it to all your data

3. how much freedom & power you give it to act on your behalf

I think for 2 & 3, security is the biggest problem. And very soon, if not already, security will become THE bottleneck for effectiveness and usefulness of AI agents as a whole (1-3), since intelligence is still rapidly scaling and is no-longer an obvious bottleneck for many use-cases.

The more data & control you give to the AI agent: (A) the more it can help you AND (B) the more it can hurt you.

A lot of tech-savvy folks are in yolo mode right now and optimizing for the former (A - usefulness) over the the latter (B - pain of cyber attacks, leaked data, etc).

I think solving the AI agent security problem is the big blocker for broad adoption. And of course, this is a specific near-term instance of the broader AI safety problem.

All that said, this is a super exciting time to be alive for developers. I constantly have agent loops running on programming & non-programming tasks. I'm actively using Claude Code, Codex, Cursor, and very carefully experimenting with OpenClaw. The only down-side is lack of sleep, and an anxious feeling that everyone feels of always being behind of latest state-of-the-art. But other than that, I'm walking around with a big smile on my face, loving life 🔥❤️

PS: By the way, if your intuition about any of the above is different, please lay out your thoughts on it. And if there are cool projects/approaches I should check out, let me know. I'm in full explore/experiment mode.

items.title: /dev/agents is out of stealth as @dreamer and is the most ambitious full stack consumer+coding agent...
items.content:
/dev/agents is out of stealth as @dreamer and is the most ambitious full stack consumer+coding agent startup I've ever seen.

when @dps first demoed this to me my jaw dropped. here's the AI Engineer POV: 

- Sidekick is an agent that builds agents and publishes on its own app store (having @hbarra cofound is p nice for that)
- unlike most appbuilders, Dreamer agents are full apps/agents - with their own MCPs, triggers, portable memory, settings, notifs, database, serverless functions, logging, prompt management, version control, terminal, etc, with a mix of official and curated LLM APIs for each task eg transcribing vs TTS
- people have been talking about the future of software and the death of the IDE. i think Dreamer is the right form factor for mass adopted personal software agents - you stop fussing over the code, you just use the app and then talk to your Sidekick to fix bugs!!
- very unexpected things happen when you let normies build their own AI apps rather than force them through expensive developers. basketball apps, knockoff harry potter galleries, storytime for kids, caltrain apps. 

sadly not an investor, just a friend of the company. but so excited to see this launch!! this is the platform for apps as a "home-cooked meal".

But worth noting that GDPval-AA is not the same thing. It only uses the public problem set, and all evaluation is done by Gemini, not by humans/specialized graders like in the real GDPval.

items.title: Compared to pretraining, finetuning an LLM doesn't take much time. This is why all frontier LLM prov...
items.content:
Compared to pretraining, finetuning an LLM doesn't take much time. This is why all frontier LLM providers replicate all good agentic ideas that Anthropic introduces first in Claude.

Given that Anthropic never tried to obscure the inner workings of its LLM and the CLI, we now see that all coding agents have the same or very similar capabilities, both closed- and open-weight.

There's still no moat. I don't know how they all are planning to survive once investor money dries out but profit margins remain nonexistent or thin.

A sign of a path forward for agents that will not terrify IT.

items.title: You don't need a "Longterm Memory MCP", just create a LEARNING md file in your project and put this ...
items.content:
You don't need a "Longterm Memory MCP", just create a LEARNING md file in your project and put this in CLAUDE md: 

- The `https://t.co/A7G4WfbNSN` file contains insights, tips, and best practices discovered during previous tasks. Read this file before running new tasks to benefit from prior knowledge. Update it with any new findings after each task run if necessary (only when new information is available or when you need to update existing information)

You have full access to the entire memory, plus you can track changes via git.

items.reasoning: Highly practical and well-received tip (262 likes, 18K views) for managing AI assistant memory. Simple, elegant approach that avoids MCP complexity. Resonated strongly with the developer community.

You don't need a "Longterm Memory MCP", just create a LEARNING md file in your project and put this in CLAUDE md:

Update it with any new findings after each task run if necessary (only when new information is available or when you need to update existing information)

You have full access to the entire memory, plus you can track changes via git.

items.title:
I haven't manually processed a piece of mail in weeks. One prompt handles all of it.  

  → categori...
items.content:
I haven't manually processed a piece of mail in weeks. One prompt handles all of it.  

  → categorizes each piece of mail 
  → compares my bills to cheaper providers 
  → flags deadlines 
  → queues payments 
  → calendars events and RSVPs 
  → auto-sorts junk (unless the credit card sign-up bonus is high enough 👀)  

Also been doing this with everything physical - started a video stream with ChatGPT in a Korean supermarket to pick the right snack, snapped a pic in an Australian pharmacy aisle to find their equivalent of Dramamine, had Claude walk me through an old washer/dryer manual based on a photo... I'm snapping photos for AI context multiple times a day.  

The physical world is AI's most underrated context opportunity and most people haven't caught on yet.  

Full prompt and workflow inside so you can copy it 👇

items.title: If i were pitching @steipete i would start by telling the Siri story: how the founders were sold by ...
items.content:
If i were pitching @steipete i would start by telling the Siri story: how the founders were sold by Steve Jobs who called them every day for a month. It was launched in my house. 

Promised them they would be distributed on iPhone.

Today it is.

If @sama ships a surveillance device with @openclaw inside? (I hear it is a device you will hold, place, or wear).

It very quickly becomes an important thing to a lot of people.

Everyone on OpenAI will want one within first three weeks. As soon as you give it a few minutes of your unbiased attenfion you will sceam in amasement.

Well then i will stand in line again. 

(I was first to buy one in the store where he walked over from his Palo Alto store, so have a little credibility).

I will attempt to do same this time with this product. It will soon know everything about me it doesn’t already know.

Scary. 

I have a packed closet that isnt fun to look into.

Still buying.

items.title:
Okay, I've seen enough. I need to call it. 

We are getting to the point where it'll be impossible t...
items.content:
Okay, I've seen enough. I need to call it. 

We are getting to the point where it'll be impossible to tell reality from fiction.

Just think about the implications.

People will send their avatars to an interview for a job. A 100% realistic avatar with the knowledge of a state-of-the-art model.

How can you tell?

Personal touch and physical presence are becoming increasingly valuable every day.

items.content:
✨ Made a new robot for my site https://t.co/2R4tRNJF0z that uses its data to find good and bad areas and mark them

It collects:
- crowdsourced drawing (like an area is tourist)
- crowdsourced tags data like "Pickpockets everywhere"
- positive places data like lots of trees, parks, playgrounds, cafes to work, coworkings
- negative places data like lots of pawn, money transfer, payday loan shops, homeless shelters or strip clubs

And then lets @xAI Grok 4.1 Fast use that data to figure out the best and worst areas of a city

I just tested it and I think it works! 😊👌

items.title: Our robot has driven me and the family for two hours in driving rain across one of USA’s most danger...
items.content:
Our robot has driven me and the family for two hours in driving rain across one of USA’s most dangerous roads.

Without my hands touching the wheel once.

It is how it handled pudddles, two brown streams crossing the road that most impresses me. While coming at us fast.

None of you will do better. It was perfect.

Which leads to trust.

Once you trust a company with your life you will buy anything that company offers.

People have no idea how good the AI is from @tesla.

Trust will sell a lot of robots.

Better. It gets me to trust all AI.

items.title:
I’ve never seen a customer agent being used in therapy centers.

But the way this AI handled the cal...
items.content:
I’ve never seen a customer agent being used in therapy centers.

But the way this AI handled the call is impressive
Voice shaky. Long pauses. Sentences trailing off. AI didn't rush or fill the silence.

That's something most humans can't even do well.

We keep talking about AI getting smarter. Better at code, images, reasoning. Nobody's talking about it getting emotionally intelligent.

Not "detecting sentiment" in a dashboard. Actually knowing when to shut up. Knowing that "Great!" is the wrong response when someone's having a rough day.
Most companies already deliver worse experiences with real agents who are overworked and burned out.

AI isn't competing with the best human on their best day. It's competing with the average Tuesday afternoon call. And it's winning.

The company that's closest to nailing this is @polyaivoice. They built the whole thing for phone calls from day one.

They already handle calls for Marriott, Gordon Ramsay's restaurants, and every major casino in Vegas.

This call is what made it click for me:

items.title:
🇪🇺 @steipete on why Europe was unable to retain him as talent:

"In the US, most people are enthusia...
items.content:
🇪🇺 @steipete on why Europe was unable to retain him as talent:

"In the US, most people are enthusiastic. 

In Europe, I get insulted, people scream REGULATION and RESPONSIBILITY.

And if I really build a company here, then I get to struggle with things like investment protection laws, employee rights, and paralyzing labor regulations.  

At OpenAI, most people work 6-7 days a week and get paid accordingly.  

In Europe, that's illegal."
items.author: @levelsio
items.summary: levelsio shares a quote from @steipete about why Europe fails to retain tech talent: enthusiasm gap, regulatory burden, labor laws making 6-7 day work weeks illegal, investment protection laws creating friction compared to US.

items.title:
Very cool community project!

🪲agent-debugger

agent-debugger is a terminal debugger for LangGraph/L...
items.content:
Very cool community project!

🪲agent-debugger

agent-debugger is a terminal debugger for LangGraph/LangChain agents. It combines agent-level visibility (state, messages, tool calls, store snapshots, and semantic breakpoints) with Python-level debugging (line breakpoints, stepping, stack, and locals) in one Textual UI.

Repo: https://t.co/meuZlN7chA
items.author: @hwchase17

items.content_html:
Very cool community project!

🪲agent-debugger

agent-debugger is a terminal debugger for LangGraph/LangChain agents. It combines agent-level visibility (state, messages, tool calls, store snapshots, and semantic breakpoints) with Python-level debugging (line breakpoints, stepping, stack, and locals) in one Textual UI.

Repo: https://t.co/meuZlN7chA

items.title:
Extracting large-scale structured information from complex PDFs is really hard, even with LLMs.

80%...
items.content:
Extracting large-scale structured information from complex PDFs is really hard, even with LLMs.

80% accuracy isn’t good enough; a lot of business applications require 98%+. You need specialized capabilities that can not only parse complex tables and charts, but also trace back to the source elements with confidence scores and citations.

We’ve created a best in class document extraction service with LlamaExtract, and you can see for yourself in ~5 mins! Simply log on to LlamaCloud and click on our templates to take a look.

Check out LlamaCloud here: https://t.co/XYZmx5TFz8

If you’re looking to productize document extraction, come talk to us: https://t.co/Ht5jwxSrQB

items.title: Your agents can work autonomously, but they still use a hardcoded API key stored in a configuration ...
items.content:
Your agents can work autonomously, but they still use a hardcoded API key stored in a configuration file.

Let that sink in.

We are building autonomous machines with access to our most critical infrastructure, and securing them with a password on a sticky note.

This is crazy.

Identity is one of the biggest problems with agents right now.

Deployed agents touch real infrastructure: databases, APIs, file systems, MCP servers, and other internal services. These agents act autonomously, make decisions, and access live resources.

The "solutions" I usually see:

• Hardcoded API keys and persistent credentials in config files
• Shared service accounts with way too many permissions
• Long-lived credentials that never get rotated
• Zero visibility into what agents accessed what data

This might work for traditional software, but agents are a completely different animal.

Agents operate continuously, make non-deterministic decisions, and follow different execution paths every time they run.

The old identity models built around static roles and long-lived secrets simply don't fit.
items.author: @svpino
items.summary: svpino highlights the problem of autonomous agents using hardcoded API keys, arguing traditional identity models don't fit non-deterministic agents that operate continuously.
items.reasoning: Identifies a genuine and under-discussed security problem with AI agents in production. The framing of agent identity as fundamentally different from traditional software identity is insightful. Good engagement (13K views).
items.summary_html: svpino highlights the problem of autonomous agents using hardcoded API keys, arguing traditional identity models don't fit non-deterministic agents that operate continuously.

items.title:
Now that I've had 24 hours to think about it, @hackwithtrees was the best of America. 

I've been to...
items.content:
Now that I've had 24 hours to think about it, @hackwithtrees was the best of America. 

I've been to every state except Alaska.

And this was the highest concetration of people building the future at a very high level.

I've been telling the stories on Spaces today. A couple of teams that are simulating forest fires and helping save lives and property (and are working with the fire fighters to build it). 

Another built an app to help seniors keep track of things. I'm heading toward that age at a rapid pace, so I loved that. 

Another taught me to dance after it learned itself by watching YouTube videos. 

I could go on for many pages. 

It deeply changed how I view the future. And even jobs. 

One team showed me an AI that would help me use my Macintosh better. 

"Why can't your AI just do it?"

"Oh, that's our goal, but it doesn't work every time yet and we need more data to finish the job off."

So it watches you as you use your computer. Freaky, huh?

But highly helpful. 

Especially navigating things that aren't easy to learn, like @Blender.

items.summary: Scobleizer recaps @hackwithtrees hackathon at Stanford, praising it as 'the best of America' with teams building AI for forest fire simulation, senior care apps, AI dance learning from YouTube, and an AI Mac assistant that watches user behavior to learn workflows.

items.title: A lot of documents are extremely dense and repetitive in information: stapled together resumes, invo...
items.content:
A lot of documents are extremely dense and repetitive in information: stapled together resumes, invoices, insurance claims, loan applications 📑

One of the main promises of AI is being able to automatically extract structured information from massive amounts of unstructured context. But in order to do this accurately over these massive documents, you need:

✅ Page attribution
✅ Bounding boxes linking back to the source text
✅ No hallucinations or dropped outputs, even if there are *hundreds* of structured fields
✅ Calibrated confidence scores to map to human review.

This is exactly what we're building with our Extract feature in LlamaCloud. Our page-level extraction lets you extract massive amounts of dense information at scale from all these documents with all the auditability and citations you need, letting humans review documents much faster than before.

https://t.co/fAstfoCNww
items.author: @jerryjliu0
items.summary: Jerry Liu details LlamaCloud Extract's capabilities for dense document processing: page attribution, bounding boxes, no hallucinations over hundreds of fields, calibrated confidence scores for human review.
items.reasoning: Technical detail about production document extraction requirements from LlamaIndex CEO. Lists concrete requirements for enterprise-grade extraction. Complements the earlier LlamaExtract post.
items.summary_html: Jerry Liu details LlamaCloud Extract's capabilities for dense document processing: page attribution, bounding boxes, no hallucinations over hundreds of fields, calibrated confidence scores for human review.

Now it will be about spe...
items.content:
After visiting robot companies in Silicon Valley this is very impressive. 

Now it will be about speeding up the supply chain. Many robot entrepreneurs explained this to me, and how hard manufacturing actually is. My friends in China told me same, getting factory up and running at speed never is easy. 

They had to study Tesla's factory to get them there. 

My bet is that Elon does it again. 

If I'm wrong, it will cost. But my expectations are still very high. He knows what's at stake.

items.title: I'm working with Teleport, and they just released their Agentic Identity Framework. They are sponsor...
items.content:
I'm working with Teleport, and they just released their Agentic Identity Framework. They are sponsoring this post.

Teleport is the solution to the identity problem for agents.

• It's a standards-driven architecture
• It gives agents proper cryptographic identities
• It scopes the agent's access at runtime
• It maintains full auditability of everything an agent does

In practice:

You have an agent that can query multiple databases, push code to repositories, and interact with internal services via MCP.

Instead of hardcoding credentials or granting the agent broad permissions, with Teleport, you'll give the agent a unique cryptographic identity and have it request just-in-time credentials scoped to the specific task.

The agent will now operate under granular access policies enforced at runtime that you can fully audit.

If you're building agents that touch production infrastructure, check this out:

https://t.co/y2HhLCX5dH
items.author: @svpino
items.summary: svpino describes Teleport's Agentic Identity Framework: gives agents cryptographic identities, scopes access at runtime, maintains full auditability. Sponsored post.

items.title: For our free newsletter this week, we talk about why LLMs won’t led to Artificial General Intelligen...
items.content:
For our free newsletter this week, we talk about why LLMs won’t led to Artificial General Intelligence (AGI).

 @IrenaCronin and I write this newsletter every week.
 
LLMs alone are unlikely to lead to AGI. They primarily predict text and can imitate planning and reasoning without reliably grounding claims, maintaining durable memory, verifying correctness, or acting safely over long time horizons. 

A more realistic path to AGI is a multi- component system where an LLM is just one part, combined with explicit planning and execution, persistent memory, verification and testing, and governance layers that constrain and audit actions once tools turn words into real world effects.

Read for free at https://t.co/HHwYy7NoAl (and please subscribe!)
items.author: @Scobleizer
items.summary: Scobleizer argues LLMs alone won't lead to AGI because they lack grounding, durable memory, verification, and safety. Proposes multi-component systems with planning, memory, verification, and governance layers.
items.reasoning: Common AGI skepticism framing that's been widely discussed. Decent engagement (16K views) but the argument is not novel. Newsletter promotion.
items.summary_html: Scobleizer argues LLMs alone won't lead to AGI because they lack grounding, durable memory, verification, and safety. Proposes multi-component systems with planning, memory, verification, and governance layers.

@IrenaCronin and I write this newsletter every week.

LLMs alone are unlikely to lead to AGI. They primarily predict text and can imitate planning and reasoning without reliably grounding claims, maintaining durable memory, verifying correctness, or acting safely over long time horizons.

A more realistic path to AGI is a multi- component system where an LLM is just one part, combined with explicit planning and execution, persistent memory, verification and testing, and governance layers that constrain and audit actions once tools turn words into real world effects.

items.title:
When I stayed on a working aircraft carrier I met many of the pilots. 

They are our best and bright...
items.content:
When I stayed on a working aircraft carrier I met many of the pilots. 

They are our best and brightest people. Often become leaders. Or heros. Remember Captain Sullenberger that landed a plane in the Hudson? 

But we should know that AI eventually will beat them. Just like it beat us in autonomous car racing in Abu Dhabi last year. Witnessed that with my own eyes.

We don't really want them to die, do we? No. They are our kids.

items.title:
This is how you ensure Agentic coding doesn't destroy your organization:

1. Process your entire cod...
items.content:
This is how you ensure Agentic coding doesn't destroy your organization:

1. Process your entire codebase and pull requests
2. Distill that knowledge into customized rules and patterns
3. Automatically track how you follow those over time

Agentic coding is the greatest thing since sliced bread, but only if you use it responsibly, maintain your codebase's strengths, and make sure your code doesn't turn ugly over time.

I didn't come up with this idea, by the way. @QodoAI just shipped this, and I think it's pretty awesome:

• They analyze your code and pull requests
• Identify repeating patterns
• Generate a set of enforceable rules

And the most important part:

You can track those over time! You'll get analytics showing which rules you are violating, how often, adoption trends, and whether your code is actually improving.

Basically, this creates a way to measure your engineering standards the same way you measure everything else.

Here is the link: https://t.co/hrP5aY4VPa

Thanks to the Qodo team for partnering with me on this post.

items.summary: Santiago Valdarrama promotes QodoAI, which analyzes codebases and PRs to distill enforceable coding rules and track engineering standards over time for agentic coding environments.

items.content:
It's definitely not a Servo clone - if it was it would be much better at rendering pages!

It reused a few libraries from Servo - most notably their HTML parser html5ever and CSS parser and selector matching library

Neither of those are rendering components, they help with parsing of text

items.title:
Over the past 6 weeks, I've automated dozens of hours of grunt, repetitive work.

Every Friday, I sp...
items.content:
Over the past 6 weeks, I've automated dozens of hours of grunt, repetitive work.

Every Friday, I spend a few minutes writing down everything I do more than once during the week:

1. I automate what I can automate
2. I improve what I can't automate

Two levels of unlock here:

1. Start using voice
2. Start using voice-to-action agents

The https://t.co/DLog1nxpeD team reached out to partner with me. I've been testing them, and this is precisely what they do.

First, if you are still typing your prompts, stop. Start using voice. Go from typing to dictation on your computer. This alone will save you hours every year.

Second, move from asking questions to using an agent that automates tasks for you.

Lemon is a VOICE-TO-ACTION agent (instead of a voice-to-text model):

• You press a button
• You say what you need
• Lemon executes the task in whatever app you are using

Lemon works across Mac apps, so you can drive them all with your voice.

Lemon is currently free. 100% worth trying.
items.author: @svpino
items.summary: svpino describes his workflow of automating repetitive tasks weekly, promotes Lemon as a voice-to-action agent that executes tasks across Mac apps via voice commands.
items.reasoning: Practical automation tips with a sponsored product (Lemon). The voice-to-action paradigm is interesting but this is largely promotional content.
items.summary_html: svpino describes his workflow of automating repetitive tasks weekly, promotes Lemon as a voice-to-action agent that executes tasks across Mac apps via voice commands.

items.title: Observability can be an "after the fact" indicator. Reactive Monitoring is not a solution, but a del...
items.content:
Observability can be an "after the fact" indicator. Reactive Monitoring is not a solution, but a delayed reaction to a problem. Ideally, especially in complex environments, the best solution is "in the moment" Autonomous Resolution. AI now makes this possible, and @NewRelic delivers!

While AI has pushed complexity beyond human scale, AI has also pushed response (turning Observability Data into Action) beyond human scale with Agentic AI.

"Agentic" means moving from a "Dashboard that shows you a spike" to a "Platform that understands and remediates it in real-time." This frees your engineers' time to focus on product innovation, not troubleshooting, to achieve business outcomes metrics with far less friction.

Smarter APM from @NewRelic is here to bridge the gap — integrating AIOps and Agentic capabilities for Autonomous Resolution.

See the next leap forward in Observability. Register now here for the #NewRelicAdvance live virtual event occurring on February 24:
https://t.co/ix2PN2PYXe

#AIOps #APM #NewRelicAdvanced

items.title:
One example stood out to me.

A Danish shipping company integrated AI into their IT operations with ...
items.content:
One example stood out to me.

A Danish shipping company integrated AI into their IT operations with a simple goal:
give time back to employees so they can focus on work that genuinely matters.

AI did not replace teams.
It removed the noise, so humans could do higher value problem solving.

This, to me, is the model for mature AI adoption.

When programming languages were released: 

1957: Fortran
1958: Lisp
1959: COBOL
1964: BASIC
1970: Pascal
1972: C
1978: SQL
1985: C++
1991: Python
1993: R
1995: PHP, Java, & JavaScript
2001: C#
2009: Go
2012: Julia
2014: Swift
2015: Rust
2016: Kotlin
2017: WebAssembly
2023: Mojo

2014: Swift

2015: Rust

2016: Kotlin

2017: WebAssembly

2023: Mojo

When AI meets an existing operational rhythm, the scale curve changes fast.

items.title:
Northern Europe has one of the highest levels of digital maturity I have seen.

That foundation is l...
items.content:
Northern Europe has one of the highest levels of digital maturity I have seen.

That foundation is letting companies leapfrog the typical AI stall patterns.

Instead of running endless proofs of concept, leaders are doing two things:

→ Prioritizing enterprise grade adoption
→ Embedding AI directly into HR, IT, and core operations

This shift is what separates hype from value.

Almost no one talks about actually scaling them.

But in Northern Europe, something different is happening.

Enterprises are quietly moving from experiments to real, operational AI that impacts the core business.

I explore this shift with @dannywilks74, Area VP, EMEA North at @ServiceNow.

Here is what surprised me most…

#ServiceNowPartner

items.title: In 1995 some government people took me up in the Pearl Tower. That is the tower with several balls a...
items.content:
In 1995 some government people took me up in the Pearl Tower. That is the tower with several balls as part as the architecture.

Literally none of this existed.

They pointed below at the Bund island and said: “this is where our Wall Street is going.”

At the time 15% of the world’s construction cranes were building the city.

I counted more than 60 skyscrapers being built at the same time.

Planning a trip back there in May.

But tomorrow @maryamie is kidnapping us and taking us to Vegas.

Which is a minor town in comparison to Shanghai.
items.author: @Scobleizer

What if your next product launch video didn’t require a camera, an editor, or a production budget?

Just text.

In my latest collaboration with @Magiclight0926, I tested a simple idea:

Can written words turn into fully sequenced, professional video in minutes?

Most executives still think video equals complexity.

They are wrong.

Here’s what’s actually changing, and why it matters.

items.title: The biggest fuckup by humanity is inventing different sign languages. And the most ridiculous part o...
items.content:
The biggest fuckup by humanity is inventing different sign languages. And the most ridiculous part of this fuckup is that American Sign Language and British Sign Language aren't even remotely similar.

What a wasted chance to unite all the people of the world with a single language, or at least let a Brit and an American with hearing problems understand one another.

items.title:
The real shift is not “AI makes videos.”

It is this:

AI now handles the invisible work.

→ Generat...
items.content:
The real shift is not “AI makes videos.”

It is this:

AI now handles the invisible work.

→ Generates visuals that match your script
→ Sequences them with motion and sound
→ Optimizes pacing automatically
→ Tests tone variations for different audiences

Behind the scenes, it runs thousands of “what if” scenarios.

That used to take teams.

Now it takes minutes.

items.title: The second #kakapo chick of the #kakapo2026 breeding season hatched this morning: Hine Taumai-A1-202...
items.content: The second #kakapo chick of the #kakapo2026 breeding season hatched this morning: Hine Taumai-A1-2026 on Ako's nest on Te Kākahu. We transferred the egg from Anchor two nights ago. This is Ako's first-ever chick, which is just a few hours old in this video. #conservation #birds #parrots
